data.description:
## What
Prove the batch write reduces commit count and does not break crash-resume: a before/after
commit-count benchmark on a synthetic many-event import, plus rollback + crash-resume tests.

## Why
Batching changes commit granularity (N events per commit). We must demonstrate the win
(commit-count reduction) and prove the safety properties: all-or-nothing per commit, and
that a crash mid-batch plus the `source_id` re-scan resumes cleanly (idempotency already
works; verify batching doesn't break partial-batch recovery).

## Scope
- Benchmark: synthetic N-event NDJSON import; assert commit count drops from ~N to
  `ceil(N/256)` (a >=10x reduction at N>=2560), exercised via a real import producing a
  real tickets branch.
- Malformed-event-mid-chunk test: one bad event in a chunk triggers whole-chunk rollback
  (no partial commit, no phantom staged blob).
- Crash-resume test: simulate a crash before the batch commit; the next run's `source_id`
  re-scan skips already-committed events and re-emits the rest — leaving whole-commit-or-none.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `tests/` benchmark asserts commit count == `ceil(events/256)` on a synthetic many-event import (>=10x fewer commits than per-event)
- [ ] Malformed-event-mid-chunk test proves whole-chunk rollback (index clean, no partial commit)
- [ ] Crash-resume test proves partial-batch recovery leaves whole-commit-or-none and the source_id re-scan resumes cleanly
- [ ] Regression suites green: replay, dedup, cross-clone union-merge convergence, SNAPSHOT compaction
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` all green
data.title: B4: commit-count benchmark + crash-resume/rollback tests for batched writes
